content:
# Jude Chapter 1 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.1 — "a servant of Jesus Christ" vs. "a slave of Jesus Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a servant of Yeshua Christos" | Ioudas Iēsou Christou doulos |
| LOGOS | "a servant of Jesus Christ" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a slave of Jesus Christ" | "Slave" for doulos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. doulos means "slave/servant" — both are valid translations. "Servant" is the traditional English rendering that preserves the honor of the title (being God's servant was a title of distinction in Israel — cf. Moses, David). "Slave" is more etymologically precise but carries modern connotations of degradation that the ancient honor-title did not. In a letter where the author introduces himself with this word as a credential, "servant" better captures the intended dignity.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Both are valid; "servant" preserves the honor-connotation.

---

### v.4 — "sensuality" vs. "live immoral lives"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "perverting the grace of our God into licentiousness" | tēn tou theou hēmōn charita metatithentes eis aselgeian |
| LOGOS | "who pervert the grace of our God into sensuality" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "saying that God's marvelous grace allows us to live immoral lives" | Adds "saying that... allows us to"; "marvelous" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says they "pervert" (metatithentes — "transfer/change") grace into aselgeia ("sensuality/licentiousness"). PNEUMA restructures this into a doctrinal claim: "saying that God's marvelous grace allows us to live immoral lives." While this is the practical implication, the author describes an action (perverting grace), not a teaching (saying grace allows immorality). PNEUMA also adds "marvelous," which is not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA converts action into doctrine.

---

### v.12 — "hidden reefs at your love feasts" vs. "dangerous reefs that can shipwreck you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] in your love feasts, feasting together without fear" | houtoi eisin hoi en tais agapais hymōn spilades syneuōchoumenoi aphobōs |
| LOGOS | "hidden reefs at your love feasts, as they feast with you without fear" | spilades = reefs/rocks |
| PNEUMA | "dangerous reefs that can shipwreck you" | Adds "shipwreck"; drops "love feasts" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. spilades means "reefs" or "rocks" — hidden dangers at the communal meal. PNEUMA adds "that can shipwreck you" (not in the text) and drops the crucial phrase "at your love feasts" (en tais agapais hymōn). The love feast (agapē meal) is the specific setting — these false teachers are present at the community's sacred meals. Dropping this detail loses the social specificity that makes the metaphor so alarming.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA drops "love feasts" and adds "shipwreck."

---

### v.24 — "to present you blameless before the presence of his glory" vs. "will bring you with great joy into his glorious presence without a single fault"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to set [you] before his glory blameless with great joy" | stēsai katenōpion tēs doxēs autou amōmous en agalliasei |
| LOGOS | "to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "will bring you with great joy into his glorious presence without a single fault" | "Glorious presence" for "presence of his glory"; "without a single fault" for "blameless" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says "before his glory" (katenōpion tēs doxēs autou) — glory is a noun, not an adjective. PNEUMA's "glorious presence" makes glory modify presence, but the author says you will stand before glory itself — the shekinah, the kabod, the radiant presence. "Without a single fault" for amōmous ("blameless/unblemished") is adequate but less resonant — amōmos is sacrificial language (an unblemished offering) that connects to the letter's concern with purity.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA converts noun to adjective.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA converts action to doctrine | 1 | "Saying grace allows" for "perverting grace into" (v.4) |
| PNEUMA drops specific details | 1 | "Love feasts" omitted (v.12) |
| PNEUMA adds narrative elements | 1 | "Shipwreck" added (v.12) |
| PNEUMA converts noun to adjective | 1 | "Glorious presence" for "presence of his glory" (v.24) |

**Overall pattern:** Jude's disagreements center on PNEUMA's tendency to explain rather than preserve. The most significant is v.12 — dropping "love feasts" removes the specific social setting that makes the false teachers' presence so dangerous. They are not just false teachers in general; they are eating at the sacred communal meal. The doxology (v.24) shows the recurring pattern of converting nouns to adjectives: "his glory" becomes "his glorious" — losing the weight of glory as a thing you stand before.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
